title: Hamas Signals Limited Police Weapons Handover; Qassam Brigades Military Arsenal Explicitly Excluded
detail: On April 19, 2026, reports emerged that Hamas officials indicated the group was prepared to hand over thousands of automatic rifles and small weapons belonging to Gaza's police force and internal security services as a goodwill gesture. However, this proposal explicitly excluded the Izz ad-Din al-Qassam Brigades — Hamas's military wing — which would retain all its rockets, drones, anti-tank missiles, and tunnel network maps. Hamas officials said they had coordinated with other Gazan security agencies on transferring some governmental control to the Board of Peace's technocratic committee. The IDF rejected any proposal to merely transfer policing weapons while the Qassam Brigades retained full military capability, calling it 'a cosmetic gesture, not disarmament.' The Jerusalem Post cited a New York Times report on the proposal, noting that Gaza residents caught between the parties expressed desire for full disarmament: 'We want Hamas to give up their weapons and the Israelis to withdraw.' The limited handover proposal was widely interpreted as a negotiating tactic rather than substantive progress on Phase Two.

media.caption: Hamas officials claim group ready to hand over small weapons, rifles — but not military arsenal — Jerusalem Post

title: Hamas Misses Second Board of Peace Disarmament Deadline; Cairo Talks Continue With No Breakthrough
detail: April 19, 2026 was the second Board of Peace deadline by which Hamas was expected to signal acceptance of the phased disarmament framework — the Saturday follow-on to the April 14 deadline that Hamas had already formally rejected. Hamas again did not accept the plan. However, the Times of Israel reported that talks were ongoing in Cairo, with Board of Peace High Representative Nickolay Mladenov, Qatar, Egypt, Turkey, and the US still engaged. Hamas's delegation publicly called for international pressure on Israel to honor remaining Phase One obligations — including full IDF withdrawal from populated areas and unrestricted humanitarian access — before any disarmament discussion. The six-month ceasefire anniversary was approaching with no meaningful reconstruction having started: less than $1 billion of $17 billion in pledged reconstruction funds had been deposited, with donors holding back pending the disarmament and governance resolution. US officials and the IDF expressed optimism that Hamas would eventually make a move — signaling that the talks, while stalled, had not fully collapsed.

media.caption: Hamas not expected to okay disarmament offer by Saturday deadline, but talks ongoing — Times of Israel
